So today's topic is output parsers and this topic is considered very important in Lang chain. So in that sense today's video is very important. Before starting the video, there is a disclaimer. Today's video is much more connected than the previous video. If you remember, I had put a video in this playlist a few days ago on the topic of structured output. Today's video is very deeply connected to that video. So I would request that if you have not seen the previous video, then first watch that video and then you will understand today's video completely. So in today's video, I will start with the fundamentals. First I will tell you what are the output parsers and what are their needs. And then I will give you the end-to-end information about the four most important output parsers in the Lang chain. How to use them, when to use them and all the related code I will provide you. So I guess you got the idea about this video. Now let's start the video. So guys, before starting the video, let's take a recap of the previous video because today's video is a little connected to the previous video. In the previous video, we talked about the structure output. I told you that whenever you talk to LLM, then the LLM response that it gives you is textual and being textual means it is unstructured. And since this response is unstructured, you cannot send it to any other system. Systems like database or API, you cannot send this unstructured response to such systems. And then the concept of structured output comes here. What do you do in structured output? You force your LLM to give you structured output instead of textual output. Structured output means giving you an output that has some type of structure, some scheme. Like you can force your LLM to give you a reply by flipping the JSON. And now if JSON comes to you, you can easily send it to a database or API. So basically the idea was that with the help of structured output, you can make your LLM talk to any other system. Like databases and API. So this was the whole idea that we discussed in the previous video. Now I told you that generally you will get two types of LLMs. There are LLMs that support by default structured output. These LLMs are fine-tuned in such a way that if you tell them that you want structured output, they will give you structured output. So this is the category of CAN. They can give you structured output. Then I told you that generally open source models that you get are not that fine-tuned to give structured output in response. So these models can't give you structured output. Then we studied a function in the lang chain to work with the models that can give structured output. We studied with the GPD models how the output works. Now today's video will be all about working with these models that can't give structured output by default. Output parsers help us in this whole thing. Which we are going to read today. How it helps, what are the different output parsers, when and who should use it. We are going to cover all this in today's video. So I really hope that before starting the video, you have cleared the agenda. Now we will discuss step by step. First of all, we will discuss what are output parsers. So here I have written a definition. Let's read the definition first. Then I will explain it to you. So output parsers in lang chain help convert raw LLM responses, textual responses into structured format like JSON, CSV, Pydantic models and more. They ensure consistency, validation and ease of use in applications. So the basic idea is that output parsers are some classes in the lang chain. With the help of which you can work with any type of LLM and derive structured output from it. Here you don't have to be confused. You can work with output parsers with the CAN models. You can use the model that gives the default structured output and also with the CAN model that does not give the default structured output. I will show you both in this video. I will work with open source models and I will work with GPT model. And you would see how seamlessly output parsers work with both types of models. Now I would like to tell you one more thing. Although there are many types of output parsers in the lang chain, we will discuss the four most used output parsers. So let me tell you about the four output parsers. The first output parser that we will see is called string output parser. This is the first output parser. Second is JSON output parser. Third is structured output parser. And the last one is Pydantic output parser. Apart from this, you will get more types of output parsers in the lang chain. Like CSV output parser, list output parser. There are different types of parsers in the lang chain to deal with different data formats. But we will focus on these four parsers because you will be using one of these in most of the use cases. So now going forward, I will pick one parser, explain it and then write its code and show you. That will be the flow of this video. So now let's talk about our first output parser, which is string output parser. String output parser is the simplest output parser. It has a very simple function. It takes the LLM response and converts it into string and gives it to you. That's it. That's all the work of string output parser. Now I will explain its working. So you must have seen in past videos that whenever we talk to any LLM, chat model, we don't get only textual response. Apart from that, we get a lot of metadata. You can see how much is the token usage, how much is the completion usage, how much is the audio tokens used. We get all this metadata by flipping it. And then what do we have to do? Systems like database or API, you cannot send this unstructured response to such systems.
And then the concept of structured output comes here.
What do you do in structured output?
You force your LLM to give you structured output instead of textual output.
Structured output means giving you an output that has some type of structure, some scheme.
Like you can force your LLM to give you a reply by flipping the JSON.
And now if JSON comes to you, you can easily send it to a database or API.
